section.title: The Altar and the Courtyard
section.sectionRationale: Moving outward from the tabernacle's inner furnishings, God now gives the pattern for the bronze altar of sacrifice and the surrounding courtyard, the outer boundary that every worshiper must cross, along with the command to keep a lamp burning continually before the LORD.
section.thesis: God instructs Moses to build a bronze altar for burnt offerings and a linen-walled courtyard around the tabernacle, and commands that pure oil keep the lamp burning before Him continually, from evening until morning.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the bronze altar of burnt offering with its horns and utensils; the courtyard of linen curtains enclosing the tabernacle; and the command for continual oil to keep the lamp burning.

media.alt: A bronze altar with horns at its corners
media.caption: The bronze altar of burnt offering stands at the entrance to the courtyard.

verses.text: “You are to build an {{altar|altar}} of acacia wood. The altar must be square, five cubits long, five cubits wide, and three cubits high.
Make a horn on each of its four corners, so that the horns are of one piece, and overlay it with bronze.
Make all its utensils of bronze, its pots for removing ashes, its shovels, its sprinkling bowls, its meat forks, and its firepans.
Construct for it a grate of bronze mesh, and make a bronze ring at each of the four corners of the mesh.
Set the grate beneath the ledge of the altar, so that the mesh comes halfway up the altar.
Additionally, make poles of acacia wood for the altar and overlay them with bronze.
The poles are to be inserted into the rings so that the poles are on two sides of the altar when it is carried.
Construct the altar with boards so that it is hollow. It is to be made just as you were shown on the mountain.

units.label: The courtyard
units.note: a hundred cubits long, fifty cubits wide

verses.text: You are also to make a courtyard for the tabernacle. On the south side of the courtyard make curtains of finely spun linen, a hundred cubits long on one side,
with twenty posts and twenty bronze bases, and silver hooks and bands on the posts.
Likewise there are to be curtains on the north side, a hundred cubits long, with twenty posts and twenty bronze bases, and with silver hooks and bands on the posts.
The curtains on the west side of the courtyard shall be fifty cubits wide, with ten posts and ten bases.
The east side of the courtyard, toward the sunrise, is to be fifty cubits wide.
Make the curtains on one side fifteen cubits long, with three posts and three bases,
and the curtains on the other side fifteen cubits long, with three posts and three bases.
The gate of the courtyard shall be twenty cubits long, with a curtain embroidered with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen. It shall have four posts and four bases.
All the posts around the courtyard shall have silver bands, silver hooks, and bronze bases.
The entire courtyard shall be a hundred cubits long and fifty cubits wide, with curtains of finely spun linen five cubits high, and with bronze bases.
All the utensils of the tabernacle for every use, including all its tent pegs and the tent pegs of the courtyard, shall be made of bronze.
And you are to command the Israelites to bring you pure oil of pressed olives for the light, to keep the lamps burning {{continually|continually}}.

In the Tent of Meeting, outside the veil that is in front of the Testimony, Aaron and his sons are to tend the lamps before the LORD from evening until morning. This is to be a permanent statute for the Israelites for the generations to come.

altar.gloss: Altar, from the root meaning to slaughter or sacrifice. This bronze altar stood at the entrance to the tabernacle courtyard, the first thing any worshiper encountered, since approach to God always required a sacrifice for sin.

continually.gloss: Continually, perpetually, without interruption. Used of the lamp that must never go out, this word marks several ongoing tabernacle practices, emphasizing that Israel's worship and God's provision for it were meant to be unceasing, not occasional.

basic.context: God now describes the bronze altar of sacrifice, the very first structure any Israelite would meet on entering the tabernacle courtyard, along with the linen walls of the courtyard itself and the command to keep a lamp burning continually before Him. Sacrifice comes before access; the outer boundary comes before the inner sanctuary.
basic.hermeneutics: These are literal building instructions for Israel's worship space, not abstract symbols to be mined for hidden meaning. The order matters here as much as the details: a worshiper met the altar of sacrifice before anything else, showing that atonement for sin was the very first requirement of approaching a holy God.
basic.originalLanguages: The mizbeach, altar, takes its name from the verb to slaughter, since sacrifice for sin stood at the very entrance to worship. The lamp was to burn tamid, continually, a small but constant reminder that God's provision and Israel's worship were never meant to lapse.
basic.typology: The bronze altar, standing between the worshiper and the tabernacle itself, points to Christ's sacrifice as the necessary first step of approach to God; no one bypasses the altar to reach the Holy Place. The continually burning lamp anticipates the unceasing light and life believers have in Christ.
basic.christ: Hebrews 13:10 speaks of believers having an altar, pointing to Christ's once for all sacrifice, which the bronze altar's repeated offerings could only foreshadow. Where this altar required constant, repeated sacrifice, Christ's single sacrifice accomplishes forever what these offerings could never finish, Hebrews 10:11-14.
basic.god: God places the altar of sacrifice first, before any other furnishing, making plain that sinful people cannot approach Him without atonement. Yet He also commands a light kept burning continually, showing His unbroken commitment to remain present and provide for His people's ongoing worship.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 10:11-14 contrasts the repeated sacrifices at this altar with Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice. Hebrews 13:10 speaks of an altar believers now have in Christ. Leviticus 24:1-4 repeats the command for continually burning lamps.
basic.oneStory: Before Israel could approach God's dwelling, they had to pass the altar of sacrifice, a constant reminder that sin must be atoned for. That altar, offered again and again, points forward to the one sacrifice of Christ, who opens the way to God once for all and keeps His light burning for His people forever.

context.literary: This chapter moves outward from the inner tabernacle structure described in chapter 26 to the courtyard that surrounds it, ending with a practical, ongoing instruction about the lampstand oil that bridges into the priestly material of chapter 28.
context.historical: The courtyard's dimensions, a hundred cubits by fifty, created a large enclosed space around a comparatively small tent, so that the bronze altar, not the tabernacle building itself, was the first and most prominent structure worshipers encountered upon entering.
context.canonical: The bronze altar and its sacrifices form the backdrop for the entire sacrificial system detailed later in Leviticus, and the continually burning lamp becomes a recurring image of unceasing worship and presence throughout Israel's history.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish, through the literal layout of Israel's worship space, that sacrifice for sin was the necessary first step of approaching a holy God, and that His provision for ongoing worship, like the burning lamp, was meant to be constant.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes the specific construction of Israel's old covenant courtyard and altar; it does not prescribe a floor plan for Christian worship spaces. Its abiding truth is that access to God requires atonement, now fulfilled once for all in Christ.

debates.question: Does the placement of the altar before the tabernacle itself carry deliberate theological significance, or is it simply practical design?

views.label: Deliberate theological ordering
views.case: Many interpreters see the altar's position at the very entrance as intentional: no one could reach the tabernacle's furnishings without first passing the place of sacrifice, teaching that atonement always precedes access to God's presence.

views.label: Primarily practical design
views.case: Others caution that ancient sanctuary layouts commonly placed the altar near the entrance for practical reasons, such as smoke and ritual flow, and that theological weight should rest on what Scripture explicitly says about sacrifice rather than inferring meaning purely from spatial arrangement.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm the same substantial truth, atonement is required before approach to God, whether read primarily from explicit sacrificial law or also from the courtyard's physical layout.

study.originalLanguages: The mizbeach stands at the courtyard's entrance, its very name tied to slaughter, showing that sacrifice was the first and necessary step of worship, not an optional add on. The lamp was to burn tamid, continually, evening until morning without fail, a small daily discipline that testified to Israel's ongoing dependence on God's provision.

typology.type: The bronze altar at the courtyard entrance, requiring sacrifice before approach
typology.fulfillment: Christ's sacrifice, the necessary and sufficient basis for anyone's approach to God, Hebrews 10:19-22.

typology.type: The continually burning lamp, tended without fail
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the unfailing light of the world, and the church's calling to shine without ceasing, Matthew 5:14-16, Revelation 1:20.

study.christ: Hebrews contrasts the repeated sacrifices offered on altars like this one, which could never take away sin, with Christ's single offering, by which He has perfected forever those being sanctified, Hebrews 10:11-14. Where every Israelite had to bring another animal to this altar again and again, believers now approach God through the one sacrifice that never needs repeating.
study.god: God requires sacrifice before any nearer approach to His presence, a serious and repeated reminder of sin's cost. At the same time, He commands that light burn before Him without interruption, evening until morning, revealing His steady, unfailing commitment to dwell with and provide for His people.

crossReferences.note: Contrasts the endlessly repeated sacrifices of the old altar with Christ's single, perfecting sacrifice.
Speaks of an altar believers have, pointing beyond the old bronze altar to Christ.
Repeats and expands the command for pure oil and a continually burning lamp.

study.oneStory: Every Israelite had to pass the bronze altar of sacrifice before drawing near to God's dwelling, and the lamp within had to burn without fail, evening until morning. That pattern of required sacrifice and unceasing light reaches its fulfillment in Christ, whose one sacrifice opens permanent access to God and whose light never goes out.

quiz.q: What distinctive feature was made on each of the altar's four corners?

options.text: A horn, of one piece with the altar.
options.note: Verse 2 specifies horns on each corner, of one piece with the altar itself.

options.text: A gold ring for carrying poles.
options.note: Rings for the poles were on the bronze grate, not horns; the horns were a separate distinctive feature at the corners.

options.text: A carved image of a cherub.
options.note: Cherubim were woven into the curtains and veil, not carved onto the altar's corners.

options.text: No distinguishing features were made.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly describes horns made on each of the four corners.

quiz.q: What material made up the courtyard's surrounding curtains?

options.text: Finely spun linen.
options.note: Verses 9 and 18 describe the courtyard walls as finely spun linen.

options.text: Goat hair, matching the tent covering.
options.note: Goat hair curtains covered the tabernacle tent itself, chapter 26; the courtyard walls were finely spun linen.

options.text: Solid bronze panels.
options.note: Bronze was used for bases and utensils, not as the material for the courtyard's curtain walls.

options.text: The courtyard had no curtains, only open space.
options.note: Verses 9 through 18 explicitly describe linen curtains enclosing the courtyard.

quiz.q: What were the overall dimensions of the courtyard, according to verse 18?

options.text: A hundred cubits long and fifty cubits wide.
options.note: Verse 18 states this exact measurement for the whole courtyard.

options.text: Fifty cubits long and twenty cubits wide.
options.note: These numbers appear for individual sides, but the overall courtyard measured a hundred by fifty cubits.

options.text: Ten cubits square.
options.note: That measurement describes the smaller ark, not the much larger courtyard.

options.text: No dimensions are given for the courtyard.
options.note: Verse 18 explicitly gives the hundred by fifty cubit measurement.

quiz.q: What oil was commanded for keeping the lamps burning?

options.text: Pure oil of pressed olives.
options.note: Verse 20 specifies this exact oil for the light.

options.text: Any available cooking oil.
options.note: The command was specifically for pure, pressed olive oil, not any generic oil.

options.text: Scented anointing oil made with spices.
options.note: The anointing oil, made with specific spices, is a separate substance described later in chapter 30.

options.text: No specific oil is commanded.
options.note: Verse 20 explicitly commands pure oil of pressed olives.

quiz.q: Who was responsible for tending the lamps from evening until morning?

options.text: Aaron and his sons.
options.note: Verse 21 assigns this ongoing duty specifically to Aaron and his sons.

options.text: The seventy elders of Israel.
options.note: The elders had a different role, seen in chapter 24; the lamp tending duty belonged to Aaron and his sons.

options.text: Bezalel and Oholiab.
options.note: Bezalel and Oholiab were the skilled craftsmen who built the tabernacle, not the ones assigned to tend the lamps.

options.text: No one was assigned this responsibility.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly assigns the duty to Aaron and his sons as a permanent statute.

questions: The bronze altar stood at the very entrance to the courtyard, before anything else could be approached. What would it look like for you to let the reality of Christ's sacrifice be the first thing you bring to mind before you try to draw near to God?
The courtyard's linen walls marked a clear boundary around holy ground. Where in your life have the boundaries around what is sacred to you become blurred or forgotten?
The lamp had to be tended continually, evening until morning, without fail. What spiritual disciplines in your life have become occasional rather than continual?

Aaron and his sons carried an ordinary, repeated responsibility, tending a lamp night after night, as part of their calling before God. How do you view the small, repeated tasks of your own daily life, work or otherwise, in relation to worship?

Every sacrifice at this altar had to be repeated again and again, yet Christ's sacrifice never needs repeating. Where are you still trying to atone for old failures rather than resting in what Christ has already finished?

translationNotes.note: The horns of the altar were prominent projections at each corner with later significance in the sacrificial system, including the application of blood to them, Leviticus 4:7, and as a place of refuge, 1 Kings 1:50-51.
Made with boards so that it is hollow indicates the altar was a wooden frame overlaid with bronze, likely filled with earth or stones for the actual fire, rather than a solid bronze block.

From evening until morning describes the lamp burning through the night; some understand the lamps as also being tended, though not necessarily burning, during the day, since 'continually' in verse 20 emphasizes uninterrupted care more than nonstop flame at every hour.
